title: French battleship Strasbourg
clean_text: {{Infobox ship |section1={{Infobox ship/image | image = Strasbourg-2.jpg | image_caption = in port }} |section2={{Infobox ship/career | country=France | flag= | name = | namesake = City of Strasbourg | ordered = 16 July 1934 | builder = | laid_down = November 1934 | launched = December 1936 | commissioned = 15 September 1938 | fate = *Scuttled on 27 November 1942 *Sold for scrap, 1955 }} |section3={{Infobox ship/characteristics | class= | displacement= *Standard: *Full load: | length = (loa) | beam = | draft = | power = * * 6 × Indret boilers | propulsion = *4 × screw propellers *4 × geared steam turbines | speed = | range = | crew = 1,381–1,431 | armament = * 8 × 330mm/50 Modèle 1931 guns * 16 × 130mm dual-purpose guns * 8 × anti-aircraft (AA) guns * 32 × 13.2mm AA machine guns | armor = * Belt: * Deck: * Turrets: * Conning tower: | aircraft =2 floatplanes | aircraft_facilities = 1 catapult }} }} was the second and final member of the of fast battleships built for the French Navy in the 1930s. She and her sister ship were designed to defeat the German of heavy cruisers that had been laid down beginning in the late 1920s, and as such were equipped with a battery of eight guns to counter the six guns of the s. was laid down in November 1934, was launched in December 1936, and was commissioned in September 1938 as the international situation in Europe was steadily deteriorating due to Nazi Germany's increasingly aggressive behavior. was assigned to the Atlantic Fleet upon entering service; her only significant peacetime activities consisted of visits to Portugal in May 1939 and a tour of Great Britain in May and June that year. She spent the next two months training with other units of the fleet and in September, France and Britain declared war on Germany over its invasion of Poland, starting World War II. The two -class ships formed the core of the (Raiding Force), tasked with protecting Allied merchant shipping in the central Atlantic. The unit was quickly divided into two groups, with and other units being detached as Force X, based in Dakar. With an increasingly belligerent Italy threatening to enter the war in April 1940, Force X was recalled to the Mediterranean to act as a deterrent, though the defeat of French forces in the Battle of France rendered the situation moot. The armistice specified that Strasbourg and the rest of the French fleet would be demilitarized, and she and several other battleships were stationed in Mers-el-Kébir at the time. Mistakenly under the impression that the Germans sought to seize the ships, the British Force H was sent to either force the ships to continue fighting or to destroy them. When the French refused the ultimatum, the British opened fire, but Strasbourg evaded the British and escaped to Toulon. There, she served as the flagship of the newly established Forces de haute mer until the Germans attempted to seize the fleet during Case Anton, leading to its destruction in the scuttling of the French fleet in Toulon in November 1942. The wreck was seized by the Italians, who raised Strasbourg and then started dismantling the ship, and was later taken by the Germans. The vessel was bombed and sunk a second time by American bombers in August 1944, and was sold for scrap in 1955. thumb|left|US Navy recognition drawing of the class The French Navy's design staff spent the decade following the 1922 Washington Naval Treaty attempting to produce a satisfactory design which would allow several ships to be built within the 70,000-ton limit allowed by the treaty. Initially, the French sought a reply to the Italian s of 1925, but all proposals were rejected. A 17,500-ton cruiser, which could have handled the s, was inadequate against the old Italian battleships, however, and the 37,000-ton battlecruiser concepts were prohibitively expensive and would jeopardize further naval limitation talks. These attempts were followed by an intermediate design for a 23,690-ton protected cruiser in 1929; it was armed with guns, armored against guns, and had a speed of . Visually, it bore a profile strikingly similar to the final -class design. The German s became the new focus for French naval architects in 1929. The design had to respect the 1930 London Naval Treaty, which limited the French to two 23,333-ton ships until 1936. Drawing upon previous work, the French developed a 23,333-ton design armed with 305 mm guns, armored against the German cruisers' guns, and with a speed of . As with the final -class design, the main artillery was concentrated entirely forward. The design was rejected by the French parliament in July 1931 and sent back for revision. The final revision grew to 26,500 tons; the 305 mm guns were replaced by /50 Modèle 1931 guns, the armor was slightly improved, and the speed slightly decreased. Parliamentary approval was granted for the first member of the class in early 1932, and was ordered on 16 July 1934 with the design unchanged but with greater armor thicknesses and increased weight. thumb|left|Inboard profile of the class displaced standard and fully loaded, with an overall length of , a beam of and a maximum draft of . She was powered by four Parsons geared steam turbines and six oil-fired Indret boilers, which developed a total of and yielded a maximum speed of . Her crew numbered between 1,381 and 1,431 officers and men.{{refn|group=Note|Whitley's Battleships of World War II provides the 1,381 figure, while Conway's All the World's Fighting Ships states the crew numbered 1,431. Garzke & Dulin confirm the 1,381 number, and further clarify that the crew was composed of 56 officers, 1,319 enlisted men, and six civilian crew members.}} The ship carried a pair of spotter aircraft on the fantail, and the aircraft facilities consisted of a steam catapult and a crane to handle the floatplanes. She was armed with eight 330mm/50 Modèle 1931 guns arranged in two quadruple gun turrets, both of which were placed in a superfiring pair forward of the superstructure. Her secondary armament consisted of sixteen /45 dual-purpose guns; these were mounted in three quadruple and two twin turrets. The quadruple turrets were placed on the stern, with one on the centerline on the superstructure and the other two on either side on the upper deck, and the twin turrets were located amidships, just forward of the funnel. Close range antiaircraft defense was provided by a battery of eight guns in twin mounts and thirty-two guns in quadruple mounts. The ship's belt armor was thick amidships, and the main battery turrets were protected by of armor plate on the faces, both thicker than installed in . The main armored deck was thick, and the conning tower had thick sides. was ordered on 16 July 1934 in response to the Italian s. The keel for the ship was laid down in November 1934 in the No. 1 slipway of the civilian Ateliers et Chantiers de la Loire shipyard in Saint-Nazaire. After her launching in December 1936, she was moored along the fitting-out quay, where her armament, propulsion system, and other equipment were installed. departed Saint-Nazaire on 15 June 1938, bound for Brest, France; while en route she conducted brief speed tests. The ship arrived on 16 June and departed again on 21 June to begin official acceptance trials. Modifications were done from 22 to 30 June, followed by more sea trials that continued into August. Gunnery trials were performed on 24–25 August off the island of Ushant, and the ship was formally commissioned on 15 September. That day, she was drydocked so that her propulsion system could be inspected after the trials. On 15 December, went to sea to resume working up for active service, including more gunnery testing. By this time, the international situation in Europe had begun to worsen as Nazi Germany began making increasingly hostile demands on its neighbors. On 24 April 1939, the ship was finally ready for active service, joining the Atlantic Fleet with her sister ship ; the two ships were designated the 1st Battle Division. The ships received identification stripes on their funnels, one for as division leader, and two for . joined on 1 May for the first time for a cruise to Lisbon, Portugal on 1 May, arriving there two days later for a celebration of the anniversary of Pedro Álvares Cabral's discovery of Brazil. The ships left Lisbon on 4 May and arrived in Brest three days later. There, they met a British squadron of warships that were visiting the port at the time. The two -class ships sortied on 23 May in company with the 4th Cruiser Division and three destroyer divisions for maneuvers held off the coast of Great Britain. and then visited a number of British ports, including Liverpool from 25 to 30 May, Oban from 31 May to 4 June, Staffa on 4 June, Loch Ewe from 5 to 7 June, Scapa Flow on 8 June, Rosyth from 9 to 14 June, before stopping in Le Havre, France from 16 to 20 June. The ships arrived back in Brest the next day. The squadron conducted more training off Brittany through July and into early August. thumb|left|Profile view of from a [[US Navy warship recognition guide]] In August, as tensions with Germany were again heightened, this time over territorial demands on Poland, the French and British navies discussed coordination in the event of war with Germany; they agreed that the French would be responsible for covering Allied shipping south from the English Channel to the Gulf of Guinea in central Africa. To protect shipping from German commerce raiders, the French created the (Raiding Force), with and as its core. The group, which was under the command of (Squadron Vice Admiral) Marcel-Bruno Gensoul, also included three light cruisers and eight large destroyers, and was based in Brest. British observers informed the French Navy that the German "pocket battleships" of the class had gone to sea in late August, bound for the Atlantic, and that contact with the vessels had been lost. On 2 September, the day after Germany invaded Poland, but before France and Britain declared war, the sortied from Brest to guard against a possible attack by the s. Upon learning that the German ships had been spotted in the North Sea, Gensoul ordered his ships to return to port after they met the French passenger liner in the Azores. The escorted the liner back to port and arrived in Brest on 6 September. By this time, the German raiders had broken out into the Atlantic, so the British and French fleets formed hunter groups to track them down; the was split, with and operating individually as Force L and Force X, respectively. , the aircraft carrier , and three cruisers remained in Brest while and two French heavy cruisers joined the British aircraft carrier , based in Dakar, French West Africa. left Brest on 7 October in company with the destroyer and a division of torpedo boats, meeting Hermes off Camaret-sur-Mer later that day. The ships then steamed south to meet the heavy cruisers off Casablanca, ultimately arriving in Dakar on 14 October. The ships went on a patrol in the central Atlantic from 23 to 29 October, during which they captured the German merchant ship Santa Fe on the 25th. Another sweep followed from 7 to 13 November to the west of Cape Verde; the ships found four Germans aboard a Belgian passenger liner and took them prisoner. Other warships arrived in Dakar on 21 November to relieve ; she left that day along with the cruiser and an escort of torpedo boats. Ordered to return to Brest, it was feared that German aircraft had dropped naval mines in the harbor entrance, so the ships were temporarily diverted to Quiberon Bay until a search revealed no mines on 29 November. Faced with increasingly hostile posturing by Italy during the spring of 1940, the was dispatched to Mers-el-Kébir on 2 April. , , two cruisers, and five destroyers left that afternoon and arrived three days later. The squadron was quickly ordered to return to Brest just a few days later in response to the German landings in Norway on 9 April. The left Mers-el-Kébir that day and arrived in Brest on the 12th, with the intention that the ships would cover convoys to reinforce Allied forces fighting in Norway. But the threat of Italian intervention continued to weigh on the French command, which reversed the ships' orders and transferred them back to Mers-el-Kébir on 24 April; and the rest of the group left that day and arrived on 27 April. They conducted training exercises in the western Mediterranean from 9 to 10 May but saw little activity for the next month. On 10 June, Italy declared war on France and Britain. Two days later, and sortied to intercept reported German and Italian ships that were incorrectly reported to be in the area. The French had received faulty intelligence that had indicated that the Germans would attempt to force a group of battleships through the Strait of Gibraltar to strengthen the Italian fleet. After the French fleet got underway, reconnaissance aircraft reported spotting an enemy fleet steaming toward Gibraltar. Supposing the aircraft to have spotted the Italian battle fleet steaming to join the Germans, the French increased speed to intercept them, only to realize that the aircraft had in fact located the French fleet. The fleet then returned to port, marking the end of s last wartime operation. On 22 June, France surrendered to Germany following the Battle of France. Under the terms of the Armistice, and would remain at Mers-el-Kébir. thumb| slips her moorings and makes for open water while under fire from the British [[Force H]] The only test in battle for came in the attack on Mers-el-Kébir on 3 July. The British, misinterpreting the terms of the armistice as providing the Germans with access to the French fleet, feared that the ships would be seized and pressed into service against them despite assurances from François Darlan, the Chief of the Naval General Staff, that if the Germans attempted to take the vessels, their crews would scuttle them. Prime Minister Winston Churchill then convinced the War Cabinet that the fleet must be neutralized or forced to re-join the war on the side of Britain. The British Force H, commanded by Admiral James Somerville and centered on the battlecruiser and the battleships , and , arrived off Mers-el-Kébir to coerce the French battleship squadron to join the British cause or scuttle their ships. The French Navy refused, as complying with the demand would have violated the armistice signed with Germany. To ensure the ships would not fall into Axis hands, the British warships opened fire at 17:55. was tied alongside the mole with her stern facing the sea, so she could not return fire. Immediately after the British started shooting, Gensoul issued the order to get underway and return fire. , at that time commanded by (Ship of the line captain) Collinet, was the first large warship to slip her mooring, following four destroyers on their way out of the harbor. Debris from near misses showered the ship as she made her way through the port; some of these fragments dented or punched holes in her hull, and burning debris scorched her deck. A salvo of shells near-missed the ship at 18:00. As the vessels cleared the jetty, the destroyers steamed first ahead of to engage British destroyers off the harbor entrance and then arrayed themselves to her port side as the ships steamed east. Heavy smoke in the harbor from the destroyed battleship , which had suffered a magazine explosion, masked the escape of and the destroyers, and Somerville was initially unaware that she had evaded his attack. Somerville had received a report from a patrolling Fairey Swordfish pilot that a -class battleship had left the harbor, but he refused to believe it, as he believed that a field of naval mines that had been laid earlier that morning would have prevented any escape. Upon clearing the harbor, the British destroyer , which had been tasked with watching the entrance, turned to lay a smoke screen to protect Force H from what appeared to be a destroyer attack. The effect was to help to obscure , which, as she increased speed from , let out a large cloud of black smoke. The French ships steered for a gap in the defensive minefield to bring themselves under the protection of a battery of coastal guns on Cape Canastel. By this time, the destroyers and had arrayed themselves ahead of the ship while and covered her to the rear. At 18:40, the two leading destroyers depth charged the British submarine , forcing her to take evasive action. At 19:00, the destroyers and and the torpedo boat arrived from Oran to help cover her withdrawal. thumb|Map of s path from Mers-el-Kébir By this time, Somerville had realized that had escaped and turned east in pursuit; he launched an air strike from the aircraft carrier , hoping to slow , while he steamed ahead with Hood, two light cruisers, and several destroyers. The first wave of aircraft, six bombers carrying bombs, attacked at 19:45; all of the aircraft missed and two were shot down by the heavy anti-aircraft fire from and the destroyers. was still belching thick, black smoke, the result of damage to one of her air intakes from fragments of the jetty; the problem could only be corrected by shutting down boiler room No. 2. This would have reduced her speed to , which was not an acceptable solution with Hood now in pursuit. By 20:25, Somerville broke off the chase, but a second flight from Ark Royal, armed with torpedoes, attacked at 20:45. As with the first strike, all of the aircraft missed. With night having fallen and Hood having disengaged, the boilers could be turned off. The thirty crewmen in the boiler room were found to have succumbed to the heat and fumes, five of whom died. Repairs to the intake were effected while still underway, and within an hour, the boilers could be used again. Collinet was still unsure whether Somerville was still pursuing him, so rather than head directly for Toulon, he steered for the south of Sardinia. Under strict radio silence to prevent Somerville from discovering his intentions, Collinet waited until was some from San Pietro Island off the coast of Sardinia at 10:00 on 4 July before turning northwest to Toulon. The ship entered the harbor there at 21:10; her crew lined the deck and she was greeted with the crews from several heavy cruisers in the harbor who cheered and played "La Marseillaise", the French national anthem. thumb|left| as she appeared in 1942 On 6 July, the French naval command ordered Gensoul to return to Toulon; he arrived and raised his flag aboard later that day. Repairs to the damage incurred during the attack were completed quickly, and in August the command reorganized the fleet. The prewar units were dissolved between 8 and 12 August; during this period, Gensoul left the vessel on the 10th. was assigned to the 3rd Squadron and on 14 August she was taken into the shipyard in Toulon to have screens installed to protect the anti-aircraft gun crews. She emerged from the yard on 11 September and two weeks later, the naval command created the (High Seas Forces), with serving as its flagship under Jean de Laborde. At the time, the fleet also included the heavy cruisers , , and and the light cruisers and . On 5 November, and all of the cruisers except sortied to receive the old battleship , which had been damaged during the attack on Mers-el-Kébir and then repaired. was escorted by a group of five destroyers, and the two units met off the Balearic Islands and arrived back in Toulon on 8 November. The next two years passed uneventfully, in large part due to the restrictions on French naval activities under the armistice with Germany. Training operations were limited to two per month, and the cruisers and destroyers had to alternate between periods of active service and reserve status with skeleton crews. went into drydock from 15 April to 3 May 1941 for periodic maintenance, and in December she steamed to visit Marseille for four days. The ship was drydocked again on 31 January 1942 for a major refit that included the installation of radar. Her anti-aircraft battery was also modified. The work was completed on 25 April and she returned to service on the 27th. She had to be drydocked for repairs that lasted for five days to her rudder motors in mid-July. thumb| after having been bombed by US Army Air Force bombers on 18 August 1944. The capsized ship is the cruiser Following Operation Torch, the Allied invasion of French North Africa on 8 November 1942, Germany launched Case Anton in retaliation, moving to seize all of the so-called "Zone libre", the part of Vichy France that had up to that point remained unoccupied. Early on 27 November, elements of the German 7th Panzer Division approached the city with the intention of seizing the ships of the . At 05:30, de Laborde issued orders to his ships' crews to scuttle their vessels. Sabotage teams had already been preparing to destroy their ships, including stuffing the gun barrels with explosives, and once the order was given, teams passed through each ship with sledgehammers, smashing equipment that might be salvaged by the Germans and Italians including rangefinders, gyrocompasses, radios, and other valuable equipment. Boiler room personnel lit the boilers and shut off the water feeds to cause them to overheat and explode. The Germans reached the port at 05:50 and issued orders to de Laborde to hand over his ships; he informed them that they were already sunk. appeared to be undamaged, leading to confusion among the German officers, but the ship's seacocks had been opened and she was in the process of sinking to the harbor bottom. At 06:20, de Laborde ordered the sabotage team aboard to detonate the scuttling charges that would destroy the ship and prevent her from being simply sealed and re-floated. Italy received control of most of the wrecks, and they decided to repair as many ships as possible for service with the Italian fleet, but to scrap those that were too badly damaged to quickly return to service. The Italians deemed to be a total loss after she was re-floated on 17 July; they accordingly began to dismantle the vessel in port. The armistice between Italy and the Allies in September 1943 stopped these activities and the ship was then seized by the Germans. On 1 April 1944 they handed her back to the Vichy French authorities. Her wreck was then towed to the Bay of Lazaret, where she was placed in a "state of conservation". She was heavily bombed and sunk by US forces—including the battleship on 18 August, three days after Operation Dragoon, the Allied invasion of southern France. She was raised for the second time on 1 October 1944 but found to be beyond repair, and used as a testbed for underwater explosions until condemned and renamed Q45 on 22 March 1955, to be sold for scrapping on 27 May that year. * {{cite book | last = Breyer | first = Siegfried | year = 1973 | title = Battleships and battle cruisers 1905–1970 | publisher = Macdonald and Jane's | location = London | isbn = 978-0-356-04191-9 | ref = }} * {{cite book |last=Dumas |first=Robert |title=Les cuirassés Dunkerque et Strasbourg |trans-title=The Battleships Dunkerque and Strasbourg |language=fr |publisher=Marine Éditions |year=2001 |location=Paris |isbn=978-2-909675-75-6 |ref= }} * {{cite book |last1=Garzke |first1=William H. Jr. |last2=Dulin |first2=Robert O. Jr. |title=British, Soviet, French, and Dutch Battleships of World War II |year=1980 |publisher=Jane's |location=London |isbn=978-0-7106-0078-3 |ref= | name-list-style=amp }} * {{cite DANFS | last = Havern | first = Christopher B. | title = Nevada II (Battleship No. 36): 1916–1948 | url = https://www.history.navy.mil/research/histories/ship-histories/danfs/n/nevada-ii.html | access-date = 29 November 2019 | date = 12 December 2016 | ref = }} * {{cite book | last1 = Jordan | first1 = John | last2 = Dumas | first2 = Robert | title = French Battleships 1922–1956 | publisher = Seaforth Publishing | isbn = 978-1-84832-034-5 | ref = | date = 2009|name-list-style=amp }} * {{cite book | last = Labayle-Couhat | first = Jean | year = 1974 | title = French Warships of World War I | publisher = Ian Allan | location = London | isbn = 978-0-7110-0445-0 | ref = }} * {{cite book | last = Roberts | first = John | chapter = France | pages = 255–279 | editor1-last = Gardiner | editor1-first = Robert | editor2-last = Chesneau | editor2-first = Roger | year = 1980 | title = Conway's All the World's Fighting Ships 1922–1946 | location = Annapolis | publisher = Naval Institute Press | isbn = 978-0-85177-146-5 | ref = | name-list-style=amp }} * {{cite book | last = Rohwer | first = Jürgen | year = 2005 | title = Chronology of the War at Sea 1939–1945 – The Naval History of World War Two | publisher = Chatham Publishing | location = London | isbn = 978-1-59114-119-8 | ref = }} * {{cite book |last=Whitley |first=M. J. |title=Battleships of World War II |year=1998 |location=Annapolis |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1-55750-184-4 |ref= }} * [http://www.maritimequest.com/warship_directory/france/battleships/strasbourg/strasbourg.htm Maritimequest Strasbourg Photo Gallery] Category:Dunkerque-class battleships Category:World War II battleships of France Category:Ships built by Ateliers et Chantiers de la Loire Category:Battleships sunk by aircraft Category:Shipwrecks of France Category:World War II shipwrecks in the Mediterranean Sea Category:1936 ships Category:World War II warships scuttled at Toulon Category:Ships sunk by US aircraft Category:Maritime incidents in November 1942 Category:Maritime incidents in August 1944

'''''Love/Hate''''' (rendered as '''''Love Me All Summer, Hate Me All Winter''''' on the front cover) is the debut [[studio album]] by American singer-songwriter [[The-Dream]]. It was released on December 11, 2007, by [[Def Jam Recordings]] and [[Radio Killa Records]] (The-Dream's [[imprint label]] through Def Jam) during his emergence as a prominent songwriter-producer in [[contemporary R&B|R&B]] and [[pop music]].

The-Dream pursued an [[electronic music|electronic]], [[avant-garde]], and [[hook (music)|hook]]-oriented direction of [[soul music|soul]] on ''Love/Hate'', drawing inspiration from the 1980s musical works of [[Prince (musician)|Prince]] and [[Michael Jackson]] as well as the [[Atlanta hip hop|rap]] forms of his native Atlanta. Recorded with fellow songwriter-producers [[Tricky Stewart]] and [[Carlos McKinney]], the album employs synthesizer, keyboard, and percussion sounds on eccentrically arranged tracks sequenced in a [[suite (music)|suite]]-like manner. It also features [[guest appearance]]s from rapper [[Fabolous]] and singer [[Rihanna]], whose 2007 hit single "[[Umbrella (song)|Umbrella]]" had been written by The-Dream and served as another template for the album. Themed mostly in sex, his quirky lyrics are [[falsetto]]-sung and half-rapped from the perspective of a lecherous romantic with [[self-awareness]] and [[Moral sense theory|moral sense]].

''Love/Hate'' was promoted with the release of three [[Single (music)|single]]s – "[[Shawty Is a 10]]", "[[Falsetto (song)|Falsetto]]", and "[[I Luv Your Girl]]" – which became [[top 40]] hits on the [[Billboard Hot 100|''Billboard'' Hot 100]]. The album itself charted at number 30 on the [[Billboard 200|''Billboard'' 200]], selling 59,000 copies in its first week in the US. It eventually reached 552,000 copies sold and was certified [[RIAA certification|Gold]] by the [[Recording Industry Association of America]] (RIAA), proving a minor success with mainstream audiences. Acclaimed on release, ''Love/Hate'' is regarded by critics as an influential and definitive R&B album from its era as well as a [[guilty pleasure]] for its qualities of [[silliness]] and [[catchiness]].

== Background ==
Born Terius Nash in 1977, [[The-Dream]] grew up in Atlanta and learned to play musical instruments in elementary school before advancing to writing lyrics during high school. Several years later, he met songwriter-producer [[Laney Stewart]] who helped procure him a [[publishing contract]], leading to successful songwriting efforts like [[Britney Spears]]' "[[Me Against the Music]]" (2003), among the many songs The-Dream would compose with Stewart's brother, fellow songwriter-producer [[Tricky Stewart|Christopher "Tricky" Stewart]]. Alongside Tricky Stewart and mutual creative partner [[Carlos McKinney|Carlos "Los Da Mystro" McKinney]], The-Dream emerged as a songwriter-producer during the decade and helped popularize an [[electronic music|electronic]] [[Pop music|pop]]-[[Contemporary R&B|R&B]] sound that followed a tradition pioneered by [[Prince (musician)|Prince]], [[Kashif (musician)|Kashif]], [[Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis]], [[Teddy Riley]], [[Timbaland]] and [[Missy Elliott]], and [[the Neptunes]].<ref name="ambio">{{cite web|last=Kellman|first=Andy|date=n.d.|url=https://www.allmusic.com/artist/the-dream-mn0001028077/biography|title=The-Dream – Biography & History|publisher=[[AllMusic]]|access-date=June 24, 2021|archive-date=March 27, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190327091539/https://www.allmusic.com/artist/the-dream-mn0001028077/biography|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2007, The-Dream and Stewart achieved a commercial breakthrough by writing [[Rihanna]]'s "[[Umbrella (song)|Umbrella]]", which topped [[record chart]]s in the US and abroad, becoming one of the era's most popular hit singles. Its success attracted songwriting offers from several [[record label]]s, including Rihanna's home-label [[Def Jam Recordings]]. While initially hesitant to sign The-Dream as a solo recording act, the label conceded after seeing his and McKinney's composition "[[Bed (J. Holiday song)|Bed]]" (2007) become a hit single for [[Capitol Records]] singer [[J. Holiday]].<ref name="ambio"/> The-Dream told [[The Island Def Jam Music Group|Island Def Jam]] executive Karen Kwak about his intentions as a recording artist, particularly the style of singing he would pursue to be successful. "I sent her the record 'Bed' and she was like, 'Crazy!'{{-"}}, he recalls.<ref name="bbsept"/>

== Writing and recording ==
[[File:Christopher Tricky Stewart.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Tricky Stewart]] (shown in 2009) co-wrote and co-produced much of the music.]]

The-Dream told ''[[SOHH]]'' in August 2007 that writing and recording for ''Love/Hate'' took a total of nine days, with twelve tracks from the recording sessions making the final cut.<ref name="SOHH"/> He conceived ''Love/Hate'' with an [[auteur]]istic approach,<ref name="MF"/> directing the album's sessions with Stewart and McKinney each contributing production for certain tracks.<ref name="booklet"/> The sessions were credited as taking place at Larrabee North in [[Universal City, California|Universal City]], Legacy Studios in New York, [[Studio at the Palms]] in Las Vegas, Triangle Sound Studios in Atlanta, and [[Westlake Recording Studios|Westlake Audio]] in Los Angeles.<ref name="booklet"/>

The-Dream wrote much of the album based on his relationship with singer and then-wife [[Nivea (singer)|Nivea]].<ref name="SOHH">{{cite web |url=http://www.sohh.com/articles/article.php/12261 |title=SOHH Soulful Exclusive: Def Jam's New Signee, The Dream, on Track to Become the Next Ne-Yo |website=[[SOHH]] |date=August 7, 2007 |access-date=September 28, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070813031650/http://www.sohh.com/articles/article.php/12261|archive-date=August 13, 2007 |url-status=dead|last=Bolden|first=Janeé}}</ref>{{refn|group=nb|Nivea confirmed in December 2007 that she and The-Dream were divorcing after rumors of a potential divorce reportedly circulated shortly prior.<ref>{{cite web|author=Sam|date=December 10, 2007|url=https://thatgrapejuice.net/2007/12/the-dream-nivea-to-divorce/|title=The Dream & Nivea to Divorce|website=That Grape Juice|access-date=July 5, 2021}}</ref>}} He credited her with inspiring more sincerity in his songwriting, "to open up that other side of it that connects with the everyday woman", as he told ''SOHH''. "She has helped me a lot with that area."<ref name="SOHH"/> Through his ambitions for the album, The-Dream wanted to provoke higher artistic standards in the music industry, telling ''SOHH'':

{{cquote|Music is uninspiring right now. The bar needs to be raised; a creative standard should be set in music. I'm hoping that the real quality in these songs shines through, and leaves a sounding impact on the listeners... It's more of what I'm giving other people. It's like the '80s; it's musical. I'm doing the 'Umbrella' routine to this whole album. All of my records are singles. The album is really visual as well. It appeals to all your senses, similar to ''[[Thriller (album)|Thriller]]'' ... very 80's, very Prince, sensual, sexy stuff... Artists are gonna have to do some homework to find out who they are.<ref name="SOHH"/>}}

== Music and lyrics ==
On ''Love/Hate'', The-Dream employs an ultramodern and [[hook (music)|hook]]-laden approach to contemporary [[soul music|soul]] with influences from past artists such as Prince, [[Michael Jackson]], and [[R. Kelly]].<ref name="MF"/> Tracks on the album are [[music sequencer|sequenced]] in the manner of a [[Suite (music)|suite]] with recurring musical elements;<ref name="Kellman"/> the layered production throughout features spacious beats,<ref name="Hinshaw"/> oscillating keyboards, throbbing synthesizers, and [[baroque music|baroque]] elements such as [[String synthesizer|synthesized strings]] and [[harpsichord]] sounds.<ref name="Kellman"/> Simon Vozick-Levinson of ''[[Entertainment Weekly]]'' characterizes The-Dream's electronic arrangements as "unorthodox",<ref name="Levinson"/> while ''[[Slant Magazine]]''{{'}}s Wilson McBee finds him influenced by "Timbaland's space jams and Prince's gleeful synth lines".<ref name="McBee"/> In another analysis, [[AllMusic]]'s Andy Kellman dubs ''Love/Hate'' "a post-Timbaland/post-Neptunes pop album" and "state-of-the art pop circa 2007–2008 [...] resolutely luminescent", with "rubbery" rhythms "sometimes colored by those swishing, panning effects heard in 'Bed' and its many imitators".<ref name="Kellman"/> [[Kelefa Sanneh]] of ''[[The New York Times]]'' notes a "gooey, robotic '80s-influenced R&B" and writes that the album embodies "the ecstatic sound of pop radio in 2007".<ref name="Sanneh">{{cite news|last=Sanneh|first=Kelefa|authorlink=Kelefa Sanneh|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/23/arts/music/23sann.html|title=Few Big Albums, but Small Ones Sounded Just Fine|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=June 26, 2021|url-access=limited|archive-date=August 17, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180817133115/https://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/23/arts/music/23sann.html|url-status=live}}</ref> According to ''[[Memphis Flyer]]'' journalist Chris Herrington, The-Dream blurs "any distinctions between mainstream and avant-garde" within the context of "black pop".<ref name="MF">{{cite magazine|last=Herrington|first=Chris|date=June 11, 2009|url=https://www.memphisflyer.com/dreams-life|title=Dream's Life|magazine=[[Memphis Flyer]]|access-date=June 24, 2021|archive-date=June 25, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625012932/https://www.memphisflyer.com/dreams-life|url-status=live}}</ref>

Lyrically, the songs are composed from the perspective of a lecherous romantic, with attitudes alternately boastful and vulnerable.<ref name="Kellman"/> Drew Hinshaw of ''[[PopMatters]]'' observes that The-Dream "engages in the same brand of improvident hedonism" as his contemporaries – "snatching [women] from their long-term relationships, cheating indiscriminately, brandishing dollars and the things they buy". However, the singer also demonstrates [[Moral sense theory|moral sense]], according to Hinshaw, who says "his nagging [[conscience]] and his ear for tragedy" come to the forefront of the songs.<ref name="Hinshaw"/> ''[[USA Today]]''{{'}}s Steve Jones observes a degree of subtlety in The-Dream's sexual suggestions.<ref name="Jones"/> Vocally, his [[phrase (music)|phrasing]] is characterized by extended syllables, [[falsetto]] frequencies,<ref name="Christgau"/> semi-rapped melodies,<ref name="pitchfork"/> and sung [[refrain]]s of "ella" and "eh" in the manner of "Umbrella".<ref name="Fennessey">{{cite magazine|last=Fennessey|first=Sean|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kCYEAAAAMBAJ&pg=PA85|title=The-Dream – Love/Hate (Def Jam)|magazine=[[Vibe (magazine)|Vibe]]|page=85|date=February 2008|via=[[Google Books]]|access-date=October 31, 2016|archive-date=June 27, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210627044822/https://books.google.com/books?id=kCYEAAAAMBAJ&pg=PA85|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Hill"/> The-Dream repurposed the "Umbrella" refrains throughout the album to serve as a signature of "notoriety" for listeners to associate with him.<ref name="jet">{{cite magazine|ref=none|last=Hoffman|first=Melody K.|date=March 31, 2008|page=46|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rzsDAAAAMBAJ&pg=PA46|title=The-Dream: Singer 'Wrote All Those' Hit Songs for Hot Stars|magazine=[[Jet (magazine)|Jet]]|access-date=June 26, 2021|via=[[Google Books]]}}</ref>{{refn|group=nb|The-Dream elaborated further to ''[[Jet (magazine)|Jet]]'' in 2008, saying, "I tried to draw those lines from each record, to each other so that everybody else knows 'he did this record.' It took people a long time; even now, when I say I wrote this record and that record, they think that either [[J. Holiday]] is copying off [[Rihanna]] or I'm copying off J. Holiday and Rihanna. Then it takes them a while to say, 'Oh no, he wrote all those records.'"<ref name="jet"/>}} Sean Fennessey of ''[[Vibe (magazine)|Vibe]]'' characterizes his songwriting as "quirky" and adds that he channels "Prince at his vampy peak, and [[Bobby Brown]], who always led with an assured growl".<ref name="Fennessey"/> In summarizing The-Dream's lyrical persona on the album, [[Robert Christgau]] muses:

{{cquote|True, he pursues other's girls, leaves one shawty because she's not quick enough on the get-down, and moves on to the speedier, needier Nikki when another doesn't immediately accept his tender offer. But mostly he just enjoys himself in bed and makes pop in the studio. In 'Luv Songs,' he does both simultaneously.<ref name="Christgau"/>}}

== Marketing and sales ==
[[File:Def Jam Recordings.svg|left|upright|thumb|Logo of [[Def Jam Recordings|Def Jam]], the album's record label]]

Around the time of "Bed"{{'}}s release, The-Dream debuted on Def Jam with the single release of "Shawty Is da Shit" (also censored as "Shawty Is a 10"). "Falsetto" was released soon after as his next single, followed by "I Luv Your Girl", the last of ''Love/Hate''{{'}}s singles.<ref name="ambio"/> All three charted in the top 10 of ''[[Billboard (magazine)|Billboard]]'' magazine's [[Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs]]<ref name="ambio"/> and the [[top 40]] of the [[Billboard Hot 100|Hot 100]] (with "Shawty Is a 10" at number 17, "Falsetto" at number 30, and "I Luv Your Girl" at number 20).<ref>{{cite magazine|url=https://www.billboard.com/artist/the-dream/chart-history/bsi/|title=The-Dream Chart History|magazine=Billboard|access-date=January 31, 2020|archive-date=April 26, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200426010047/https://www.billboard.com/music/the-dream/chart-history/BSI|url-status=live}}</ref> In September 2007, ''Billboard'' reported that the album was scheduled for a December 11 release through Island Def Jam,<ref name="bbsept">{{cite magazine|last=Concepcion|first=Mariel |url=http://www.billboard.com/articles/news/1048832/umbrella-writer-the-dream-preps-solo-debut |title=Umbrella' Writer The-Dream Preps Solo Debut |magazine=[[Billboard (magazine)|Billboard]] |date=August 7, 2007 |access-date=September 28, 2007 |archive-date=June 18, 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140618114915/http://www.billboard.com/articles/news/1048832/umbrella-writer-the-dream-preps-solo-debut |url-status=live }}</ref> although it would actually be released on Def Jam and [[Radio Killa Records]] (an [[imprint label]] created by The-Dream through the record company).<ref>{{cite web|author=Anon.|date=January 8, 2014|url=http://www.mtv.com/news/1720112/the-dream-leaves-def-jam-records/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150806231323/http://www.mtv.com/news/1720112/the-dream-leaves-def-jam-records/|url-status=dead|archive-date=August 6, 2015|title=The-Dream Says Goodbye to Def Jam Records|website=[[MTV News]]|access-date=July 2, 2021}}</ref>

In the August 2007 interview for ''SOHH'', The-Dream revealed the title ''Love/Hate'' as an abbreviation for "love me all summer, hate me all winter", explaining that "because they love you when you hot and when you're cold they don't ... I'm hot right now and they love me, but I was cold and they wasn't fuckin' with me."<ref name="SOHH"/> The album cover was revealed to ''[[Rap-Up]]'' on November 14, featuring the full title.<ref>{{cite magazine|author=Anon.|url=https://www.rap-up.com/2007/11/14/new-album-covers-the-dream-and-ghostface-killah/|title=New Album Covers: The-Dream and Ghostface Killah|magazine=[[Rap-Up]]|access-date=January 31, 2020|date=November 14, 2007|archive-date=February 1, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200201010137/https://www.rap-up.com/2007/11/14/new-album-covers-the-dream-and-ghostface-killah/|url-status=live}}</ref> Years later, The-Dream elaborated further on the title, saying it had been based on the "in's and out's of life", positive and negative qualities in people, and "how everything [relates] to a carnival."<ref name="Smith">{{cite magazine|last=Smith|first=Da'Shan|date=December 11, 2017|url=https://www.billboard.com/music/rb-hip-hop/the-dream-interview-love-hate-10-year-anniversary-8063589/|title=The-Dream Breaks Down Debut Album 'Love Hate' for 10-Year Anniversary|magazine=Billboard|access-date=January 31, 2020|archive-date=February 15, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200215062922/https://www.billboard.com/articles/columns/hip-hop/8063589/the-dream-interview-love-hate-10-year-anniversary|url-status=live}}</ref>

''Love/Hate'' debuted at number 30 on the [[Billboard 200|''Billboard'' 200]] and sold 59,000 copies in its first week – the week ending December 17, 2007.<ref name="Billboard">{{cite magazine|last=Hasty|first=Katie|url=http://www.billboard.com/articles/news/1047006/groban-remains-no-1-becomes-07s-best-seller |title=Groban Remains No. 1, Becomes '07's Best-Seller |magazine=Billboard |date=December 17, 2007 |access-date=December 19, 2007 |archive-date=February 23, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160223011248/http://www.billboard.com/articles/news/1047006/groban-remains-no-1-becomes-07s-best-seller |url-status=live }}</ref> It also reached number five on the [[Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums]].<ref name="ambio"/> In 2008, The-Dream supported the album further as an opening act on [[Mary J. Blige]] and [[Jay-Z]]'s [[Heart of the City Tour]],<ref name="bbmay"/> although his performances were overlooked in the press.<ref name="nyt">{{cite news|last=Maerz|first=Melissa|date=March 4, 2009|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/03/08/arts/music/08maer.html|title=Not Content Just to Write the Hits|newspaper=The New York Times|access-date=July 2, 2021|url-access=limited|archive-url=https://archive.today/20210702090409/https://www.nytimes.com/2009/03/08/arts/music/08maer.html|archive-date=July 2, 2021|url-status=live}}</ref> Meanwhile, ''Love/Hate''{{'}}s hit singles remained popular through the year,<ref name="style">{{cite magazine|author=Anon.|date=February 17, 2009|url=http://stylemagazine.com/news/2009/feb/17/the-dream-completes-second-album-love-vs-money/|title=The-Dream Completes Second Album, Love vs. Money, Set for March 10th Release on Radio Killa/Def Jam|magazine=Houston Style Magazine|access-date=July 2, 2021}}</ref> including abroad, with "Shawty Is a 10" and "Falsetto" charting in New Zealand at number 36 and 38, respectively.<ref>{{cite magazine|ref=none|author=Anon.|date=July 3, 2020|volume=122|issue=26|page=11|title=Release of the Week: Love Story – The-Dream Completes Album Trilogy with 'Love King'|magazine=Billboard|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WWxbO-SGG7AC&pg=PA11|access-date=June 26, 2021|via=Google Books}}</ref> On July 24, the album was certified [[RIAA certification|Gold]] by the [[Recording Industry Association of America]] (RIAA), denoting 500,000 units recorded in the US.<ref name="RIAA"/> By May 2009, it had sold 552,000 copies, according to [[MRC Data|Nielsen Soundscan]].<ref name="bbmay">{{cite magazine|last=Concepcion|first=Mariel|url=https://www.billboard.com/articles/news/268752/the-dream-keyshia-cole-plan-tour|date=May 5, 2009|title=The-Dream, Keyshia Cole Plan Tour|magazine=Billboard|access-date=June 26, 2021|archive-date=June 27, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210627002204/https://www.billboard.com/articles/news/268752/the-dream-keyshia-cole-plan-tour|url-status=live}}</ref>

''Love/Hate'' was met with critical acclaim.<ref>{{cite magazine|last=Hova|first=Tray|date=June 6, 2011|url=https://www.vibe.com/2011/06/dream-hints-double-single-dropping-tomorrow-prepares-nikki-chronicles-book|title=The-Dream Hints at Double-Single Dropping Tomorrow; Prepares 'Nikki: The Chronicles' Book|magazine=[[Vibe (magazine)|Vibe]]|access-date=January 31, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200201010138/https://www.vibe.com/2011/06/dream-hints-double-single-dropping-tomorrow-prepares-nikki-chronicles-book|archive-date=February 1, 2020|url-status=dead}}</ref> Reviewing for AllMusic, Kellman applauded its "unified sound" as one "unlike most modern R&B albums", before adding that neither Timbaland nor the Neptunes have "put together something as consistent or tautly constructed, simultaneously single-oriented and album-oriented, as this".<ref name="Kellman"/> Fennessey, writing in ''Vibe'', believed the album "never breaks stride, balancing pace with power",<ref name="Fennessey"/> while ''[[Rolling Stone]]'' magazine's Christian Hoard called it "one of the most likeable R&B records of the year" because of The-Dream's instinct for composing melodies.<ref name="Hoard"/> Drew Hinshaw from ''PopMatters'' found his lyrics empathic and credited him for possessing "something few hitmakers can claim: a wide-angle lens".<ref name="Hinshaw"/> Christgau, in his review for ''[[MSN Music]]'', called ''Love/Hate'' "an utterly slight, utterly captivating R&B album" constructed from The-Dream's "extended-syllable trick, dollops of falsetto, male backups going hey and stuff, and the good nature of someone who figures there's no point being mean when you're lucky".<ref name="Christgau"/>

Some reviewers had reservations. McBee wrote in ''Slant Magazine'' that there are moments of "greatness and plenty of potential" along with "some riskless, by-the-book slow jams" from The-Dream, who nonetheless shows "a meticulous, consistent sonic arrangement".<ref name="McBee"/> Rebecca Barry Hill of ''[[The New Zealand Herald]]'' considered the "Umbrella"-style vocal refrains and various references to sex partners a [[running gag]] throughout the album, while musing that The-Dream's "closest relationship is with his synthesizers", questioning to what degree the vocals of the album were electronically processed. However, she remained impressed by the quality and interest level of the production, praising "Falsetto" and "Ditch That..." in particular, before conceding that ''Love/Hate'' is a [[guilty pleasure]].<ref name="Hill"/>

== Legacy and influence ==
[[File:The-Dream performing.jpg|upright|thumb|The-Dream onstage in New York, September 2008]]

The-Dream felt disappointed in ''Love/Hate''{{'}}s minor success with mainstream audiences, particularly its lack of nominations for the [[51st Annual Grammy Awards]] in 2009.<ref name="nyt"/> Writing in May that year, Christgau observed how the album had "yielded R&B hits [and] went Gold", but "failed to make him as ubiquitous as [[Akon]]", his contemporary in the genre.<ref name="xgau2">{{cite magazine|last=Christgau|first=Robert|date=May 2009|url=http://www.robertchristgau.com/xg/bl/thedream-09.php|title=The-Dream: Love vs. Money|magazine=[[Blender (magazine)|Blender]]|access-date=June 24, 2021|via=robertchristgau.com|archive-date=June 25, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625011943/http://www.robertchristgau.com/xg/bl/thedream-09.php|url-status=live}}</ref> According to ''The New York Times'' reporter Melissa Maerz, The-Dream was "one of pop's most reliable hit makers" but "not yet a pop star", and had also been "criticized for an uncharismatic stage presence". Attributing this outcome to marketing, The-Dream pursued a greater [[Crossover music|crossover]] strategy with his second album ''[[Love vs. Money (The-Dream album)|Love vs. Money]]'' (2009), which was written during his divorce from Nivea and explored the depreciating effect of wealth on relationships through confessional and emotionally-charged narratives, alongside guest appearances by high-profile vocalists in [[Mariah Carey]], [[Kanye West]], and [[Lil Jon]].<ref name="nyt"/>

According to Kellman, ''Love/Hate'' was the first in The-Dream's series of "melodically rich and impeccably layered" hit-R&B albums that would include ''Love vs. Money'' and ''[[Love King]]'' (2010), constituting some of the genre's "most adventurous" albums during the early-21st century.<ref name="ambio"/> After the release of ''Love vs. Money'', Christgau opined in ''[[Blender (magazine)|Blender]]'' that ''Love/Hate'' had been a "gloriously eccentric sex album" and possibly enough from the musician, while naming "Falsetto" an "'Umbrella' clone" that was the album's "signature moment".<ref name="xgau2"/> Concurrently, The-Dream achieved further success as a songwriter on some of the era's most successful pop singles, such as Carey's "[[Touch My Body]]" (2008), [[Beyoncé]]'s "[[Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)]]" (2008), and [[Justin Bieber]]'s "[[Baby (Justin Bieber song)|Baby]]" (2010), along with frequent collaborations with rap artists, as on West's "[[All of the Lights]]" (2011) and the West–Jay-Z song "[[No Church in the Wild]]" (2012).<ref name="ambio"/> Herrington reported around this time that The-Dream had risen to the forefront of the "songwriter/producer-as-auteur paradigm" in [[black music]] forged by Timbaland, the Neptunes, and West, while making R&B albums equaled only by contemporary [[neo soul]] acts such as [[Jill Scott (singer)|Jill Scott]], [[Erykah Badu]], and [[Raphael Saadiq]].<ref name="MF"/>

''Love/Hate'' proved highly innovative in its fusion of traditional R&B and [[Atlanta hip hop|Atlanta-based rap]] forms and attitudes. ''[[Pitchfork (website)|Pitchfork]]'' journalist Meaghan Garvey says it was "a defining moment for the collision of rap and R&B, a pillar of technical songwriting and soulful expression" whose influence "bled into the fabric of popular R&B".<ref name="pitchfork"/> In a 2012 review, ''[[Sputnikmusic]]'' staff writer Trebor proclaims ''Love/Hate'' "the best pop/R&B album of the 21st century" and distinct from other works in the genre for demonstrating a [[self-awareness]] amidst the silly narratives and profusion of [[catchiness|catchy]] sounds, leading him to also call it "the definition of a guilty pleasure".<ref name="sput"/> Reflecting on its 10th anniversary in 2017, ''Billboard''{{'}}s Da'Shan Smith points to the album as "the chapter closer for a landmark year, which saw hip-hop and R&B formulating into the sound and culture that we know it as today".<ref name="Smith"/>
